:::: {.stat-row}
::: {.stat-card}
**~40×**
input reduction
(0.7k vs 28k tokens)
:::
::: {.stat-card}
**~44 s**
end to end,
2 tool calls
:::
::: {.stat-card}
**~5 kcal/mol**
the error a "converged"
job was hiding
:::
::::

- **The miss wasn't a reading failure.** The local model *printed* 32.004 and still said "looks good": it lacked the threshold. The MCP ships the threshold; the model only relays it.
- **Frontier alone wasn't reliable either.** Opus caught it once, waved off the same number once, lowballed it both times. A deterministic digest beats frontier variance on judgments with a right answer.
- **The digest stays small as outputs grow.** This file: 113 KB. Opt/freq outputs: megabytes. The digest: roughly constant.
- **The real payoff is scale.** Nobody spots this on file 73 of 400. A small local model plus this MCP triages all 400.

::: {.highlight-box}
The thick-tool slide, with receipts: a small local model matched a frontier model at ~1/40th the tokens, because the thinking was already in the tool.
:::
:::

<!-- Example 2, REAL captured run (2026-06-11): Goose desktop agent +
     meetingtool MCP + Nemotron 3 Super (120B A12B) rented from NVIDIA's
     hosted endpoint. Three synthetic animal-shelter weekly meetings
     (week1-3.wav) + adoptable-pets-oct13.csv in ~/test/test. Transcription
     (WhisperX) + diarization ran locally; transcripts + summary written to
     meeting_transcripts_and_summary.txt. Hero: "where was Mittens
     mentioned" answered with Week-3 timestamps. Session JSON:
     "Meeting Summary Pet Data.json". -->
## Example 2 — three meetings, interrogated {.slide-data}

::: {.text-sm}

Three shelter meetings as audio, plus a CSV of adoptable animals. The brief to Goose: transcribe all three (meetingtool), fold in the CSV, write a summary file. New stack: desktop agent, NVIDIA-hosted open-weight Nemotron, transcription and diarization local. (Flip: the polling loop → the summary.)

::: {.r-stack}
::: {.fragment .fade-out fragment-index=0}
<img src="assets/goose-run-cascade-crop.png" style="max-width:100%; max-height:430px; width:auto; height:auto; border-radius:6px;" alt="Goose driving meetingtool: Get Status polls with sleep 20 between them, then Get Transcript once a job finishes">
:::
::: {.fragment .fade-in fragment-index=0}
<img src="assets/goose-summary-meeting3-crop.png" style="max-width:100%; max-height:430px; width:auto; height:auto; border-radius:6px;" alt="The summary's Meeting 3 section: 8 adoptions including Mittens (CSV A-0041) adopted by a retired couple from Glenbrook, with the nvidia/nemotron-3-super model chip in the input bar">
:::
:::
:::

## Example 2 — "where was Mittens mentioned?" {.slide-data}

::: {.text-sm}

::: {.center-content}
<img src="assets/goose-mittens-qa-crop.png" style="max-width:100%; max-height:330px; width:auto; height:auto;" alt="The follow-up question and the answer: Week 3 transcript quoted at [00:10] and [00:23] with speaker labels">
:::

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="58%"}

- The answer quotes the Week 3 tape at **[00:10]** and **[00:23]**, with speakers; Weeks 1–2 only *planned* to feature her. The timestamp is the citation
- **The same shape at scale:** hundreds of interview recordings plus CVs, "which candidates mentioned web-design experience?" → the relevant clips
- The audio never left the Mac; only transcript text went to the model

:::
::: {.column width="42%"}
::: {.highlight-box}
The AI doesn't pick the candidate or approve the adoption. It finds the moment in the tape so a human can judge it. AI doesn't replace the work; it makes the work faster.
:::
:::
::::
:::

## Generic AI gives generic answers.

::: {.text-md}
:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="58%"}

1. Generic AI is often *worse* than no AI for environment-specific questions: it confidently points users down wrong paths
2. The fix isn't smarter AI. It's AI that knows your setup
3. Encode "how things actually work here" into a tool → a tier-0 support layer with *correct* answers. This is where sysadmins and consultants add enormous value
4. It stops the "AI is wrong" spiral: one bad answer, and someone resists AI for everything

:::
::: {.column width="42%"}
::: {.highlight-box}
You hold the knowledge that makes AI useful here. Encoding it into tools isn't a threat to your role. It's a force multiplier for it.
:::
:::
::::
:::

<!-- Example 4: office MCP via OpenCode (CLI) + LM Studio endpoint. REAL
     captured run (2026-06-10): test_memo.docx with planted defects (HTML
     entity, unbolded deadline, slop paragraph, typo). Assets:
     office-memo-before/after.png, office-sidebyside-midrun.png (hero),
     office-opencode-trace.png (full trace). Backup of the bad memo:
     ~/Downloads/test_memo.backup.docx for re-runs. -->
## Example 3 — Word doc tool {.slide-code}

::: {.text-sm}

The brief to OpenCode, in a terminal: fix the broken `&#x2014;` entity, bold the deadline, rewrite the corporate-filler closing with `human-writer`, fix typos. Model: a 30B local Qwen via LM Studio. Result: 311 → 222 words; the 48-word "synergistic alignment" closing became two sentences.

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.accent-box}
**Before**
:::
::: {.center-content}
<img src="assets/office-memo-before-crop.png" style="max-width:100%; max-height:350px; width:auto; height:auto;" alt="The memo before, cropped to the three defects: HTML entity in the RE: line, unbolded deadline paragraph, 48-word corporate filler closing">
:::
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.highlight-box}
**After**
:::
::: {.center-content}
<img src="assets/office-memo-after-crop.png" style="max-width:100%; max-height:350px; width:auto; height:auto;" alt="The memo after, same regions: real em dash in the RE: line, bolded deadline sentence, two-sentence closing">
:::
:::
::::
:::

## Example 3 — the run, live {.slide-code}

::: {.text-sm}
:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="62%"}

::: {.r-stack}
::: {.fragment .fade-out fragment-index=0}
<img src="assets/office-sidebyside-midrun.png" style="max-width:100%; max-height:480px; width:auto; height:auto;" alt="Mid-run: OpenCode's tool calls on the left, the Word document with the em dash already fixed on the right">
:::
::: {.fragment .fade-in fragment-index=0}
<img src="assets/office-opencode-trace.png" style="max-width:100%; max-height:480px; width:auto; height:auto;" alt="The full OpenCode trace: four numbered fixes, tool calls, verification reads, and the closing change summary">
:::
:::

::: {.text-xs}
Mid-run (arrow flips to the full trace): fix #1 announced, `word_replace_paragraph` called, and the RE: line already has its em dash.
:::

:::
::: {.column width="38%"}

- **Read → edit → verify.** It reads the paragraph, edits it, then reads it back before moving on. The MCP is built around that loop; the model just follows it
- The edits land in the *open document*, live. Not a copy, not a diff to apply later
- It even took a screenshot to check its work, then noted this model can't see images and verified by reading the text back instead

::: {.highlight-box}
Every piece of this talk in one run: an app I didn't write (OpenCode), a model I host (LM Studio's endpoint), and a tool and a skill I did write (office MCP + human-writer).
:::
:::
::::
:::

<!-- Example 4 continued: Excel, REAL captured runs (2026-06-10). Same office
     MCP, four-task work order on company_data_q2_2026.xlsx (planted: gap
     column needed, misaligned floating block on Expenses, duplicate-name trap
     on Team Directory, dates stored as text). Run 1: Nemotron 3 Super via
     local vLLM + OpenCode — stuck on task 1 for ~10 min (formula refs after
     the column insert), wrote "test" into A1 to debug itself; captured as
     excel-nemotron-struggle.png and used as the control. Run 2: Claude Code +
     Opus — all four tasks, live SUMIF/DATEVALUE formulas, amber-flagged the
     two distinct Priya Nairs with a footnote, reported side effects.
     Workbook backups: company_data_q2_2026.{backup,pristine}.xlsx. -->
## Example 3 continued — the Excel tool {.slide-data}

::: {.text-sm}

Same office MCP, bigger ask, four sheets: a computed "Gap to Target" column with misses in red; a category summary table; a misaligned block to fix; a duplicate-name trap plus tenure from hire dates. Agent: Claude Code with a frontier model, on *thin* tools (read range, set cell, set formula).

::: {.r-stack}
::: {.fragment .fade-out fragment-index=0}
<img src="assets/excel-before-expenses-crop.png" style="max-width:100%; max-height:360px; width:auto; height:auto;" alt="Expenses before: a floating category block misaligned in column H">
:::
::: {.fragment .fade-in-then-out fragment-index=0}
<img src="assets/excel-after-expenses-crop.png" style="max-width:100%; max-height:360px; width:auto; height:auto;" alt="Expenses after: floating block removed, Expense Breakdown by Category rebuilt under the main table with a % of Total column">
:::
::: {.fragment .fade-in-then-out fragment-index=1}
<img src="assets/excel-after-sales-crop.png" style="max-width:100%; max-height:360px; width:auto; height:auto;" alt="Sales Data after: Gap to Target column, currency formatted, missed-target reps flagged red">
:::
::: {.fragment .fade-in fragment-index=2}
<img src="assets/excel-after-team-crop.png" style="max-width:100%; max-height:360px; width:auto; height:auto;" alt="Team Directory after: Tenure (Years) column, both Priya Nair rows amber-flagged with a footnote">
:::
:::

::: {.text-xs}
Arrow keys flip: Expenses before (the floating block in column H) → Expenses rebuilt with % of Total → Sales with the gap column and red flags → Team Directory with tenure and the amber-flagged name collision.
:::
:::

## Example 3 continued {.slide-data}

::: {.text-sm}
:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}

::: {.r-stack}
::: {.fragment .fade-out fragment-index=0}
<img src="assets/excel-sidebyside-midrun.png" style="max-width:100%; max-height:430px; width:auto; height:auto;" alt="Mid-run: Claude Code reasoning about the Priya Nair duplicates and text-stored dates while the gap column appears in Excel">
:::
::: {.fragment .fade-in-then-out fragment-index=0}
<img src="assets/excel-claude-trace.png" style="max-width:100%; max-height:430px; width:auto; height:auto;" alt="The final report: per-tab summary of changes plus two side effects worth noting">
:::
::: {.fragment .fade-in fragment-index=1}
<img src="assets/excel-nemotron-struggle.png" style="max-width:100%; max-height:430px; width:auto; height:auto;" alt="The control: Nemotron 3 Super on the same MCP and prompt, stuck cycling formula references and writing test into A1">
:::
:::

:::
::: {.column width="40%"}

- **It noticed what the prompt didn't say.** Two Priya Nairs, different emails/regions/titles → amber-flagged both with a footnote instead of deleting one. Dates stored as text → tested `DATEVALUE` in a scratch cell first
- **Live formulas, checked totals.** SUMIF tables that tie out to the sheet's own totals
- **Reported back like a worker:** per-tab summary plus "two minor side effects worth noting"

::: {.highlight-box}
**The last flip is the control run:** same MCP, same prompt, a mid-size local model — stuck on task 1, writing "test" into A1 to debug itself. Thin tools put the intelligence in the model; frontier carries it, mid-size drowns.
:::
:::
::::
:::

<!-- How this deck was made: real Positron window capture (2026-06-11).
     Skim live (~30 s): one arrow press pops the four labels. The labels are
     .shot-label chips positioned in percent coords over the screenshot. -->
## How this deck was made: AI as co-editor {.slide-code}

::: {.text-sm}
This deck, included: the context written once, then one targeted ask at a time, rendered live. Co-editor, not author.

::: {.center-content}
::: {.annotated-shot}
<img src="assets/positron-coediting.png" style="max-width:94%; max-height:560px; width:auto; height:auto; border-radius:6px;" alt="Positron IDE: context md files in the explorer, presentation.qmd in the editor, Claude Code in the terminal with a targeted edit request, and the live slide preview on the right">

::: {.fragment}
[**Context, written once** — AGENTS.md, slide-patterns.md, content-design.md, custom.scss]{.shot-label style="left:1%; top:56%;"}
[**The deck is markdown** — presentation.qmd, just a text file]{.shot-label style="left:23%; top:13%;"}
[**The agent** — Claude Code in the editor's terminal: one targeted ask, not "make me a talk"]{.shot-label style="left:24%; top:74%;"}
[**Live preview** — every edit renders where I can judge it]{.shot-label style="left:67%; top:62%;"}
:::
:::
:::
:::

# The Real Insight {background-color="#1C6D72"}

<!-- Sketch 56 + the two-builders illustration, merged. -->
## Use AI where you're already strong

::: {.text-md}
:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

**AI amplifies what you bring.**

- Bring expertise → it amplifies expertise
- Bring nothing → it amplifies your mistakes, and you can't tell when it's off

The gut check before delegating: **can I tell a good output from a bad one?**

- Yes → go; you'll catch the mistakes
- No → step back, or learn enough to judge it first

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

**"Build me a gaming website," two builders:**

::: {.accent-box}
**Non-developer** → a site that *looks* done. No auth, no real database, no payments. A demo, not a product.
:::

::: {.highlight-box}
**Web developer** → asks for auth, a hosted DB, billing, validation. Same AI; the prompt carries the foresight. The expert's edge is knowing what to ask for.
:::

That foresight is the difference between a **production product** and **vaporware**.

:::
::::

::: {.highlight-box}
**Not a "stay in your lane" rule.** Outside your expertise, slow down: learn as you go, and interact with the AI more through planning and discussion before delegating the doing.
:::
:::

<!-- Sketch 57-59 merged: keep the workflow, notice the autopilot work. -->
## Don't change your workflow for AI

::: {.text-md}
:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="58%"}

Goals, outputs, standards: unchanged. What changes: how fast specific steps happen.

- **The common failure:** reshape the workflow around AI → new workflow is worse → drop AI, stay skeptical
- **The AI-ripe parts are the ones you've stopped seeing:** the 20-minute Tuesday task, the formatting step, the reply you've sent fifty times

:::
::: {.column width="42%"}
::: {.accent-box}
**AI adapts to you, not the other way around.** Stop the reshape. Insert AI into what already works.
:::

::: {.highlight-box}
Learning to see your own autopilot work is the real skill. Attention, not creativity.
:::
:::
::::
:::

# Summary {background-color="#1C6D72"}

<!-- Landing: takeaways + first-week on-ramp on one slide, the bet underneath. -->
## Summing things up {.slide-flow}

::: {.text-sm}
:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}

**The whole talk:**

1. "AI" is a **model** plus everything wrapped around it. The wrapping (context, tools, skills) is the part you control, and what makes it yours.
2. Model and app are separate choices. "Local" means you control the data flow, not that it runs on a laptop.
3. **Tools** and **skills** move the thinking from runtime to design time. That's why a small local model is enough on your own turf.
4. AI amplifies what you bring. Use it where you can judge the output, and stay the manager.

:::
::: {.column width="45%"}

**What to try out:**

::: {.steps}
1. **Install LM Studio**, download a model the catalog says fits your machine
2. **Chat with it.** Learn what it handles well and where it gets lost
3. **Drag in a folder of documents**, ask questions, try different model sizes
4. **Write one skill, find one tool** for a simple, repetitive task you actually do
:::
:::
::::

::: {.highlight-box}
The bet of this talk: a small local model with your context, tools, and skills beats a frontier model flying blind, on the work that's specific to you. Lead AI. Don't follow it.
:::
:::

<!-- Summary apps: every app from the talk in one checkmark matrix.
     Rows ordered chat → agents → engines so the categories read top-down. -->
## Every app from this talk, one table {.slide-data}

::: {.text-xs .table-compact}

| App | Chat UI | Agent loop | Desktop GUI | CLI | IDE | Hosts models |
|-----|:-------:|:----------:|:-----------:|:---:|:---:|:------------:|
| [ChatGPT](https://chatgpt.com) | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | | |
| [Open WebUI](https://github.com/open-webui/open-webui) | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | | | | |
| [LibreChat](https://librechat.ai) | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | | | | |
| [AnythingLLM](https://anythingllm.com) | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | | |
| [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | [✓]{.pill-yes} (desktop version) | [✓]{.pill-yes} (CLI version) | | |
| [Codex](https://openai.com/codex) | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | [✓]{.pill-yes} (desktop version) | [✓]{.pill-yes} (CLI version) | | |
| [Goose](https://goose-docs.ai) | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | [✓]{.pill-yes} | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | |
| [openwork](https://github.com/different-ai/openwork) | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | | |
| [OpenCode](https://opencode.ai) | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | |
| [Copilot CLI](https://github.com/features/copilot/cli) | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | |
| [Crush](https://github.com/charmbracelet/crush) | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | |
| [pi](https://pi.dev) | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | |
| [Gemini CLI](https://github.com/google-gemini/gemini-cli) | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | |
| [Qwen Code](https://github.com/QwenLM/qwen-code) | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | |
| [Antigravity 2.0](https://antigravity.google) | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | [✓]{.pill-yes} (IDE version) | |
| [Cursor](https://cursor.com) | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | |
| [VS Code](https://code.visualstudio.com) | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | |
| [Positron](https://positron.posit.co) | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | |
| [LM Studio](https://lmstudio.ai) | [✓]{.pill-yes} | [✓]{.pill-yes} | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | | [✓]{.pill-yes} |
| [Ollama](https://ollama.com) | [✓]{.pill-partial} (minimal) | | | [✓]{.pill-yes} | | [✓]{.pill-yes} |
| [vLLM](https://docs.vllm.ai) | | | | | | [✓]{.pill-yes} |
| [llama.cpp](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp) | [✓]{.pill-partial} (minimal) | | | | | [✓]{.pill-yes} |

:::

## Questions? {.slide-data}

::: {.text-md}
**These slides** (read the HTML version; the long files scroll): [charlespeterson.dev/oarc_llm_talk](https://charlespeterson.dev/oarc_llm_talk) · source: [github.com/charliecpeterson/oarc_llm_talk](https://github.com/charliecpeterson/oarc_llm_talk)
:::

::: {.text-sm}
:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.center-content}
<img src="assets/questions-photo-1.jpg" style="max-width:100%; max-height:420px; width:auto; height:auto; border-radius:6px;" alt="An orange cat lying upside down on the desk in front of the monitor">
:::
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.center-content}
<img src="assets/questions-photo-2.jpg" style="max-width:100%; max-height:420px; width:auto; height:auto; border-radius:6px;" alt="Two cats sprawled across the desk and typing arm, mid-edit">
:::
:::
::::

::: {.text-xs}
*In-house reviewers. Every slide shipped past these two; they approve before it acts.*
:::
:::

<!-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
     REFERENCE TAIL
     Genuine overflow that didn't earn a spot in the main flow. With the deck
     now dense throughout, this is short. These are normal slides (skim or skip
     live); they're here for the download-and-read audience.
     ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->

# Extra slides {background-color="#1C6D72"}

## When NOT to use AI

::: {.text-md}
:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}

- Decisions about people (hiring, evaluation) as the *decider*, not the *research assistant*
- High-stakes output you can't verify
- Anything where you can't tell a good answer from a bad one
- Tasks outside your expertise where you're hoping AI covers the gap

:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.accent-box}
AI will not make up for a lack of skill. It enhances the skills you already have.
:::
:::
::::
:::

## The failure mode: confidently wrong

::: {.text-sm}

No tool, no context → the model fills gaps with plausible text: function signatures that don't exist, citations never written, commands that don't apply to your system. Not lying; predicting with nothing to anchor on.

::: {.center-content}
<img src="assets/lmstudio-wrong-cloud-crop.png" style="max-width:96%; width:auto; height:auto; border-radius:6px;" alt="A local Qwen model insisting it runs on Alibaba Cloud servers while running entirely on a Mac">
:::

**A live specimen:** Qwen 3.6, running entirely on my Mac with the network idle, "corrects" me about where it's running. Its training data says it's a cloud assistant, so that's the gap-filler it reaches for.

::: {.highlight-box}
Tools, skills, and KBs remove the gaps it would otherwise fill.
:::
:::

<!-- Read-later depth: what an agent app actually does before running a command.
     Generic mechanics (no unverified vendor exploits). Moved here from the
     Section 2 security block to keep the live run at four slides. -->
## Under the hood: how an agent gates a command {.slide-code}

::: {.text-sm}

The model never runs anything. It sends a *structured request*; the app runs a battery of checks before acting, and never silently rewrites your command.

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}

**Before a command runs, a good agent app:**

- Treats the request as untrusted, not an order to obey
- Checks your allow / ask / deny rules; read-only by default
- Analyzes the whole command for injection, not just string-matches
- Is operator-aware: an allow rule for `safe-cmd *` does *not* authorize `safe-cmd && rm -rf` — chained commands re-prompt
- Blocks risky fetchers (curl, wget) by default
- Fails closed: anything unmatched or uncertain goes to a human
- Shows a plain-language summary so you approve with understanding

:::
::: {.column width="45%"}

::: {.accent-box}
**Best-effort, not guarantees.** These are pattern-and-language checks, and clever inputs have slipped past them in practice. Treat them as a layer, not the boundary.
:::

::: {.highlight-box}
The deterministic boundary is what actually caps the blast radius: a sandbox, a dev container, a VM. **Sandbox first, permissions second.** Don't disable the prompts outside a box.
:::

:::
::::
:::

<!-- Privilege escalation / agentic identity. Source: IBM Technology, "AI
     Privilege Escalation: Agentic Identity & Prompt Injection Risks"
     (youtube.com/watch?v=xHJ0_Vm7lK8). Deployment-grade companion to
     "Containing it": that slide is personal use; this one is for anyone
     wiring agents into shared systems. Moved here from Section 2. -->
## The agent is a new user on your systems {.slide-compute}

::: {.text-sm}
:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

**How privilege escalation happens with agents:**

- **Over-permission ("super agency")** — an agent wired into everything is a master key; whoever can steer it holds the key
- **Privilege inheritance** — a user with no access borrows the agent's permissions; a compromised account lends the agent *its* elevated ones; either direction escalates
- **Prompt injection** — the steering mechanism from the security slides, aimed at permissions
- **Misconfiguration** — the boring classic; still the most-exploited path in any system, agentic or not

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

**The fixes, if you deploy agents for others:**

- **Least privilege, computed twice** — effective permission = the lesser of what the *user* may do and what the *agent* may do
- **No self-granted access** — an independent policy point decides what the agent reaches (an IdP for agents); tools re-verify each call
- **Context-scoped** — a read task gets read-only access, even if the agent *could* write
- **Short-lived credentials** — a tricked model can't leak a token worth replaying
- **Monitor and revoke** — watch the flows; pull access when one goes out of bounds

:::
::::

::: {.highlight-box}
The manager rule again: a new hire gets *their own account*, narrow permissions, and an audit trail, not your password. Same for the agent.
:::
:::

<!-- Practical containment: defaults, your levers, prompt injection, and the
     build-a-tool foresight point. Swapped with "The bigger threat", which now
     carries the security beat in the main flow. -->
## Containing it: defaults, levers, the new risk {.slide-compute}

::: {.text-sm}
:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Good agentic apps ship with guardrails on:**

- Ask permission before each action (the LM Studio "approve" dialog)
- Sandboxed or containerized execution
- Scoped to a working directory
- Allow / deny command lists; read-only unless told otherwise

**Your levers (least privilege):**

- Don't blanket auto-approve ("YOLO mode")
- Run in a sandbox, VM, or container
- Scope to a project dir; keep credentials out of reach

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

::: {.accent-box}
**Prompt injection — the one permissions don't fix.** An agent that reads untrusted input (a web page, a file, an email) can be *instructed* by it. Guardrails limit the blast radius, not the gullibility. Don't point an auto-approving agent at untrusted data.
:::

::: {.callout-warning appearance="simple"}
### Build a tool? The foresight is yours
A tool or MCP is a capability you hand the app: scope it tight, validate inputs, guard destructive ops. The developer decides what the AI can reach.
:::

:::
::::
:::

## Cost at scale: a back-of-envelope

::: {.text-md}
<!-- PLACEHOLDER: real numbers. Suggested shape below — fill before the talk.
     API: $/1M tokens × tokens/researcher/day × researchers × days.
     Local: GPU node amortized + power. The point is the crossover. -->
::: {.callout-warning appearance="simple"}
### Needs your numbers
A defensible API-vs-local crossover for a realistic campus workload. Without real figures this slide is hand-waving — leave it out rather than guess.
:::
:::

## Hosted open-source: the middle ground

::: {.text-md}
- **What:** open-weight models on someone else's cloud (Together AI, Fireworks, Groq, Ollama Cloud)
- **The trade:** you don't control the hardware, but the model is portable; lock-in far lower than a frontier provider
- **When:** no local hardware available, but ToS lock-in is the concern
:::

## Signs a workflow is AI-ripe {.slide-flow}

::: {.text-md}
- You do it more than ~20 times a year
- The steps are the same each time
- You can tell instantly whether the output is right
- It's mechanical enough that you've stopped noticing you do it
- The hard part is the *doing*, not the *deciding*
:::

<!-- The fourth control tier (Control the Voice), moved here from the main
     flow and merged to one slide: fine-tuning teaches style, never facts. -->
## Control the Voice: style, not facts {.slide-compute}

::: {.text-sm}
:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}

The most technical tier, simple idea: I collected samples of my own writing and trained a LoRA with Unsloth. The model now writes close to my voice instead of the instantly-recognizable "AI" style.

- **System prompt** = "write casually, short sentences" → the model approximates
- **LoRA** = the model has *seen* your writing and learned the patterns → a much better match

::: {.accent-box}
**The expensive mistake:** fine-tuning is for **style, format, and tone**, never for teaching facts. Want it to *know* your documents? RAG or tools, not training.
:::

:::
::: {.column width="45%"}

<!-- PLACEHOLDER: a couple of real before/after lines, or a redacted sample of
     your training data format (jsonl). Keep it short; this isn't the focus. -->
::: {.scroll-box}
```text
PROMPT-ONLY (generic model):
"In today's fast-paced computing
landscape, leveraging local LLMs can
unlock transformative value..."

LoRA (trained on my writing):
"Run the model where you control the
data. That's the whole point."
```
:::
::: {.text-xs}
*Placeholder — replace with real before/after.*
:::

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
### Manager note
A LoRA teaches the worker your writing voice. It is not how they learn what's in the filing cabinet.
:::
:::
::::
:::

<!-- Image gen: the same model/app/control split, different modality. Moved
     here from Control the Behavior; restore once the ComfyUI screenshots
     exist if it earns its spot back. -->
## Image generation: the same control story {.slide-data}

::: {.text-md}
:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.accent-box}
**Cloud image apps** (ChatGPT/DALL·E, Midjourney)
:::

- Locked to their app: a few fixed sizes, no transparency, weak or missing inpainting and upscaling
- The app rewrites your prompt before the model sees it; you can't inspect or disable the rewrite
- One lever (the prompt), so iterating means re-rolling and hoping

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.highlight-box}
**ComfyUI** (local, open source, node-based)
:::

- You wire the pipeline yourself: prompt → generate → inpaint → upscale, any order, any size
- Any open-weight image model; every intermediate image is inspectable
- A workflow is a file: save it, share it, rerun it. The Krita plugin paints with it live

:::
::::

::: {.highlight-box}
Same lesson as the chat apps: cloud apps hide the workflow; ComfyUI *is* the workflow.
:::
:::

## Two ComfyUI workflows, same model, same prompt {.slide-data}

::: {.text-sm}
:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.center-content}
<!-- PLACEHOLDER: <img src="assets/comfyui-simple.png" style="max-width:100%; max-height:420px; width:auto; height:auto;"> simple node graph + its output image -->
*Placeholder: simple workflow (prompt → sample → save) and its output*
:::
**Simple graph.** Prompt in, image out. This is what a cloud service does, except you picked the model and the size.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.center-content}
<!-- PLACEHOLDER: <img src="assets/comfyui-complex.png" style="max-width:100%; max-height:420px; width:auto; height:auto;"> multi-stage node graph + its output image -->
*Placeholder: multi-stage workflow (refine → inpaint → upscale) and its output*
:::
**Designed graph.** Adds a refinement pass, targeted inpainting, and an upscale stage. The better image came from a better workflow, not a better prompt.
:::
::::

::: {.highlight-box}
The manager rule, in pictures: the quality lives in the workflow you design, and the workflow is a file you keep.
:::
:::

<!-- Windows-setup progression, one slide: three ways to think about the same
     problem, payoff in the highlight box. Sets up the scaffolding slide. -->
## One problem, three ways to think about it {.slide-flow}

::: {.text-sm}
Task: set up new Windows machines: profiles and software. Same problem, three shapes of thinking:

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
::: {.accent-box}
**#1 — the copy-paste loop**
:::

Open ChatGPT. Ask for a command, run it, paste the output back, repeat. Works; slow and error-prone, and exactly what most people mean by "using AI."
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
::: {.highlight-box}
**#2 — agent on every machine**
:::

Claude Code or Codex on the machine, a skills file for the procedure; the AI works in place. Faster, but every endpoint now needs an agent, model access, credentials.
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
::: {.highlight-box}
**#3 — build the artifact, then leave**
:::

Codex builds a real `.exe`: every step, with verification and logging. Drop it on any machine. **AI built the artifact; the artifact runs without AI.**
:::
::::

::: {.highlight-box}
Chatbot → assistant → coworker that builds the solution. Sometimes the right outcome is a deterministic tool with no AI at runtime. The question for this section: what should the AI *build* for you?
:::
:::

<!-- Sketch 46-48: Hoffman2 — the punchline. Intro slide folded into the
     lead-in; the error comparison carries the example. -->
## Example 3 — same error, different outcome {.slide-cluster}

::: {.text-sm}

**The MCP:** Claude Code plus tools that know Hoffman2's *actual* queues, modules, and environment. **The test:** a missing-library error.

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

::: {.accent-box}
**ChatGPT:** "Just `sudo apt install libfoo-dev` and rerun."
:::

You can't sudo on a shared HPC node. You try variants. You burn two hours. You email support anyway, convinced "AI is useless."

<!-- PLACEHOLDER: optional real ChatGPT answer to a real Hoffman2 error. -->
::: {.scroll-box}
```text
> Error: libfoo.so.2: cannot open shared
  object file: No such file or directory

ChatGPT: This means the library isn't
installed. Install it with:
  sudo apt-get install libfoo-dev
Then re-run your program.
```
:::

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

::: {.highlight-box}
**Hoffman2 MCP:** "That's provided by the `foo/2.3.1` module. Add `module load foo/2.3.1` before your executable."
:::

Right context, right answer, in one shot. And it knows when to tell you to email support instead.

<!-- PLACEHOLDER: real Hoffman2 MCP answer to the same error. -->
::: {.scroll-box}
```text
> Error: libfoo.so.2: cannot open shared
  object file: No such file or directory

Hoffman2 MCP: libfoo is provided by the
`foo/2.3.1` module on Hoffman2. Add this to
your submission script before the executable:

    module load foo/2.3.1

Need a newer version than what's available?
Here's the install-to-home pattern ARC
supports — and here's when to email support
instead.
```
:::

:::
::::
:::

::: {.notes}
Don't rush this slide. "I bet some of you have seen exactly this happen." Pause. Let it sit.
:::

<!-- Sketch 49-51: daily-work cluster -->
## Not just research: three daily-work tools {.slide-flow}

::: {.text-sm}
:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
::: {.highlight-box}
**This talk**
:::
Quarto skeleton + CSS theme + a SKILL with my slide conventions. Claude Code renders and iterates; I drive content. Runs on Sonnet, Opus, *or* a local model.
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
::: {.highlight-box}
**Obsidian vault**
:::
An MCP that organizes notes, surfaces connections, and tightens writing in my vault. Cloud for heavy lifting, local for private notes.
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
::: {.highlight-box}
**ComfyUI images**
:::
Cloud image tools: fixed sizes, no alpha, no inpainting. ComfyUI is node-based: *you design the workflow*. Local, in Krita, fully yours.
:::
::::

::: {.highlight-box}
Same pattern every time, even when it isn't text: the tool holds my creativity and my environment-specific knowledge; the AI executes a workflow I designed. That's why a smaller local model is often enough. The thinking is already baked in.
:::
:::
